figures.name: Deus sive Natura (God or Nature)
figures.gloss: The radical equivalence of God and the natural universe. God is not a creator who stands outside of existence, but the infinite, eternal, and necessary reality of existence itself, identical with the laws of physics and the cosmos.
figures.facet: It destroys the transcendent, personal deity entirely. God has no will, no purpose, no judgments, and no emotional attachments; God is simply the totality of what is, demanding understanding rather than worship.

figures.name: The One Substance
figures.gloss: The single, indivisible foundation of all reality. Everything that exists—mind, matter, galaxies, and thoughts—are merely transient modifications or 'modes' of this singular, infinite essence.
figures.facet: It abolishes the Cartesian mind-body divide. Mind and matter are not separate substances, but parallel attributes of the exact same underlying reality, forever entwined and fundamentally identical.

figures.name: Natura Naturans (Natureing Nature)
figures.gloss: Nature in its active, generative, and infinite capacity. It is the uncaused cause, the self-generating engine of existence operating purely through the necessity of its own eternal laws.
figures.facet: It completely replaces 'creation ex nihilo' (creation out of nothing). Reality was never authored at a point in time; it is ceaselessly and necessarily unfolding from its own infinite nature.
figures.provenance: Spinoza's 'Ethics', borrowing from Medieval Scholasticism but radically secularized.

figures.name: Natura Naturata (Natured Nature)
figures.gloss: The passive, manifested universe. It is the totality of all specific, finite things—individual organisms, planets, thoughts, and objects—that result inevitably from the laws of Natura Naturans.
figures.facet: It posits absolute determinism. Every single mode—every human action, thought, and physical event—is strictly determined by preceding causes, leaving absolutely no room for human free will.

figures.name: Conatus
figures.gloss: The innate, striving force by which everything endeavors to persist in its own being. For rocks, it is inertia; for living things, it is the drive to survive; for humans, it is desire and self-preservation.
figures.facet: It grounds all ethics in biological and physical reality. Good and evil are not absolute cosmic laws, but merely subjective human labels for what aids or obstructs our specific 'conatus'.
figures.provenance: Spinoza's 'Ethics'; parallels in Thomas Hobbes and modern evolutionary biology.

figures.name: Amor Dei Intellectualis
figures.gloss: The 'intellectual love of God'—the highest human joy, achieved by rationally comprehending the necessity of all things and our precise place within the infinite web of Nature.
figures.facet: It is a love that expects no love in return. God/Nature has no emotions, so to love God is simply to accept the universe exactly as it is, without demanding it care for you.

figures.name: Gaia (The Living Earth)
figures.gloss: The Earth understood as a complex, self-regulating, and synergistic organism. The biosphere, atmosphere, oceans, and soils interact mechanically and chemically to maintain optimal conditions for life to persist.
figures.facet: It resurrects the ancient Earth Goddess as a purely scientific, cybernetic system. It requires no teleology or conscious intent; life regulates the planet blindly but effectively through feedback loops.
figures.provenance: James Lovelock and Lynn Margulis's 'Gaia Hypothesis' (1970s).

figures.name: The Web of Life
figures.gloss: The deep ecological principle that all biological life is fundamentally entangled. Organisms have no isolated existence; their reality is entirely defined by their relationships within the broader ecosystem.
figures.facet: It forces a radical biocentrism, completely displacing human beings from the apex of creation. A human is merely one strand in the web, possessing no greater inherent right to exist than a microbe.
figures.provenance: Arne Naess's 'Deep Ecology' (1973); modern environmental pantheism.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: They are the active and passive faces of the exact same infinite reality.
Intra-tradition bond: Substance is the ontological structure; Deus sive Natura is the theological realization of it.
Intra-tradition bond: Amor Dei Intellectualis is the highest, most rational expression of human conatus striving for empowerment.
Intra-tradition bond: Gaia is the macro-organism, while the Web of Life describes its internal, interdependent mechanics.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Spinoza explicitly redefined the Abrahamic Yahweh into a mechanistic, non-personal universe, triggering his excommunication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lovelock explicitly named his scientific hypothesis after the Greek Earth Goddess, syncretizing myth into cybernetic ecology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a single, infinite, all-encompassing reality that constitutes the entirety of existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both describe a single, dynamic energy that is identical to the cosmos itself, constantly generating reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the uncaused, spontaneous generative force that effortlessly produces the myriad things.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both signify the infinite, unknowable, and limitless foundation from which all finite attributes emanate.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both describe the absolute, indivisible singularity from which all varied existence is formed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize an inherent, irreducible life-force or striving essence fundamentally present within entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Spinoza's realization that the individual self is merely a transient mode aligns with the Buddhist dissolution of the ego into the interdependent whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent ultimate liberation achieved through perfect, unclouded understanding of reality's true nature.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision the animating force not as a personified ruler, but as the great generative essence running through all things.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both view the Earth not as dead matter to be exploited, but as a living entity demanding respect and maintaining ecological balance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize the planet as a total, self-sustaining living system that integrates all biological and geological processes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both frame reality as an intricate system of balance, where disrupting one strand violently disrupts the harmony of the whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both visualize the unbreakable circle of interdependence linking all forms of life and natural phenomena.

proposedArchetypes.name: Absolute Monism
proposedArchetypes.gloss: The radical assertion that only one substance or essence exists, eliminating all dualism between creator/creation, mind/matter, or sacred/profane.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Necessary for traditions that explicitly deny any ontological separation between the divine and the material universe.

proposedArchetypes.name: Intellectual Ecstasy
proposedArchetypes.gloss: The attainment of spiritual liberation or highest bliss purely through the rational comprehension of the universe, rather than through faith, ritual, or shamanic trance.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Captures the philosophical path to salvation where pure logic and deductive understanding serve as the supreme mystical experience.

proposedArchetypes.name: Inherent Vitality
proposedArchetypes.gloss: The innate, blind striving of all matter and life to persist and express its nature, acting as the fundamental driving force of existence.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Bridges Western concepts like conatus with Eastern and Indigenous concepts of pervasive, non-anthropomorphic life-force.
